title: Algebraicity of Spin L-functions for GSp_6
speaker: Ellen Eischen
abstract: I will discuss recent results for algebraicity of critical values of Spin L-functions for GSp_6. I will also discuss ongoing work toward the construction of p-adic L-functions interpolating these values. I will explain how this work fits into the context of earlier developments, while also indicating where new technical challenges arise. This is joint work with Giovanni Rosso and Shrenik Shah. All who are curious about this topic are welcome at this talk, even without prior experience with Spin L-functions.

proof_stages.label: Automorphic strategy for special-value algebraicity
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames algebraicity of critical automorphic L-values as a problem to be attacked by realizing L-values as pairings with Eisenstein series whose Fourier coefficients have algebraic or p-adic control.

proof_stages.label: Known terrain and the spin-L gap
proof_stages.summary: Previously successful standard-L-function cases for unitary and symplectic groups are contrasted with the much less developed theory of spin L-functions for GSp_{2g}, motivating the GSp6 case.

proof_stages.label: GSp6 algebraicity theorem
proof_stages.summary: For a cuspidal automorphic representation of GSp6 attached to a holomorphic scalar-weight Siegel cusp eigenform of weight 2r, the normalized critical spin values for integers s from 4 to r-2 are stated to lie in the field generated by the Fourier coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Pollack integral representation as the initial reduction
proof_stages.summary: A completed spin L-value is expressed, using Pollack's integral representation extended beyond level one, as an integral over GSp6 pairing the cusp form with a suitable Eisenstein series.

proof_stages.label: Half-spin ambient group and embedded GSp6
proof_stages.summary: The Eisenstein series lives on a larger half-spin group of type D6 built from quaternionic Hermitian matrices, and one must understand how GSp6 and its symmetric domain sit inside this group so that restriction back to GSp6 is possible.

proof_stages.label: Fourier expansion of the exceptional Eisenstein series
proof_stages.summary: The Eisenstein series is decomposed by the rank of its quaternionic Hermitian Fourier indices, treated with differential operators and results of Shimura, Gan, Martin, Carl, and Kim to obtain rationality and interpolation properties of its Fourier coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Algebraicity after pullback to GSp6
proof_stages.summary: The holomorphy and convergence range, the shifted integral representation, and the algebraic Fourier coefficients after restriction to GSp6 combine with the standard Petersson-pairing argument to give precisely the stated range of algebraic critical values.

proof_stages.label: P-adic refinements and remaining obstacles
proof_stages.summary: The construction is designed to support p-adic families and character twists, but nonunique local models, Euler-factor computations, level issues, and the ad hoc relation to other spin-L results remain obstacles to a full p-adic theory.

examples.name: Kummer congruences via p-stabilized Eisenstein series
examples.note: Serre's recovery of Kummer congruences from congruences of Fourier coefficients of Eisenstein series is used to reveal the guiding idea that algebraic or p-adic properties of modular-form coefficients can control special values of L-functions.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker writes the Kummer congruence with its hypotheses, explains Serre’s q-expansion strategy, and concretely applies it to p-stabilized weight-2k Eisenstein series via their divisor-sum coefficients.

examples.name: Rankin-Selberg convolution as a Petersson pairing
examples.note: Shimura's treatment of Rankin-Selberg convolutions of modular forms supplies the second motivating pattern: write the L-value as a Petersson pairing with an Eisenstein series and extract algebraicity from the spectral decomposition and algebraic Fourier coefficients.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker formulates the Rankin–Selberg convolution, rewrites it as a Petersson pairing with an Eisenstein series, and traces how decomposition along the eigenform extracts the L-value coefficient.
